meta.notes: El Salvador's safety situation has transformed dramatically under President Bukele's crackdown on gangs (2022-present). Advisory level dropped from 4 to 2. Safety sections reflect current improved reality while noting ongoing considerations.

language_and_money.english_proficiency: Low — limited outside upscale hotels, surf camps, and San Salvador business areas. Growing in tourist-facing surf towns like El Tunco and El Zonte.
language_and_money.cash_vs_card: El Salvador uses USD — no conversion needed for American travelers. Cards accepted at upscale restaurants, hotels, and larger businesses in San Salvador. Cash essential in surf towns, markets, and rural areas. Bitcoin legally accepted but practical use remains limited to Bitcoin Beach (El Zonte) and some tourist businesses.
language_and_money.atm_availability: Available in San Salvador and larger towns. Limited in surf towns and rural areas — withdraw before heading to the coast. Banco Agrícola and Scotiabank most reliable.
language_and_money.tipping_culture: Not mandatory but appreciated. 10% tip in restaurants is generous. Growing tip culture in tourist areas mirroring US norms.
language_and_money.tipping_etiquette: 10% in sit-down restaurants. $5-10/day for surf instructors and guides. $1-2 for hotel staff. Round up taxi fares. No tip required at counters.

visa_requirements.us_citizen: No visa required for stays up to 90 days (CA-4 agreement)

entry_and_border.passport_validity_rule: Must be valid for at least 6 months beyond date of entry
entry_and_border.customs_restrictions: Standard restrictions on narcotics, firearms, and agricultural products. Declaration required for cash over $10,000.
entry_and_border.duty_free_allowances: 400 cigarettes or 50 cigars, 2.5 liters of alcohol, gifts up to $500 value

health.malaria_risk: Very low — malaria essentially eliminated in El Salvador. Not a practical concern for tourists.
health.malaria_prophylaxis_info: Not recommended — malaria risk negligible. El Salvador is one of the few Central American countries where malaria is not a concern.
health.dengue_risk: Moderate — dengue present nationwide, particularly in urban areas and lowlands during rainy season (May-November). Regular outbreaks in San Salvador.
health.altitude_sickness_risk: Very low — highest point (Santa Ana Volcano, 2,381m) is manageable with gradual ascent. Not a practical concern.
health.food_water_safety: Tap water not safe to drink. Always use bottled or purified water. Street food generally safe at busy, high-turnover pupusería stalls. Exercise standard caution with raw foods.
health.air_quality_concerns: San Salvador has traffic-related air pollution. Volcanic activity near Santa Ana and Izalco can produce sulfur dioxide — check conditions before visiting.
health.pharmacy_access: Good in San Salvador — pharmacies (Farmacias Similares, Farmacias San Nicolás) widely available. Available in major towns. Limited in rural and surf coast areas.
health.medical_care_quality: Limited — private clinics in San Salvador (Hospital de Diagnóstico, Clínica Médica Panamericana) offer adequate care. Very limited outside the capital. Evacuate serious conditions to the US or Costa Rica.

risk_indicators: Dramatically improved security since 2022 gang crackdown under Bukele
State of exception measures remain in place — civil liberties curtailed
Residual gang activity in some areas
Ocean rip currents a major hazard at surf beaches
safety.crime_risk: Low to moderate — El Salvador's transformation is one of the most dramatic security improvements in recent history. Once one of the world's most dangerous countries, the Bukele government's mass gang crackdown (2022) has reduced homicides by over 90%. Tourist areas are now genuinely safe. Exercise normal urban precautions.
safety.scam_risk: Low — taxi overcharging, inflated tourist menus in San Salvador
safety.transport_safety: Taxis and ride-hailing safe in San Salvador. Intercity buses (first-class) safe on main routes. Avoid night travel on secondary roads. Surf town shuttle services widely used and safe.
safety.solo_female_travel_notes: Significantly safer than pre-2022. Surf towns like El Tunco and El Zonte are very manageable solo. Exercise normal precautions in San Salvador. Night walking in well-lit tourist areas is now reasonable. Still avoid isolated areas alone.
safety.lgbtq_notes: Same-sex relationships legal but no legal recognition of partnerships. Social conservatism strong — strongly Catholic country. No anti-discrimination protections. Discretion recommended outside San Salvador. Growing tolerance in surf towns among international crowd.
safety.night_safety: San Salvador tourist districts (Zona Rosa, Escalón, Santa Elena) safe at night with normal precautions. Surf towns (El Tunco, El Zonte) safe at night in established areas. Avoid poorly lit areas and non-tourist neighborhoods.
areas_to_avoid: Downtown San Salvador (historic center) at night
Soyapango and Mejicanos (San Salvador outskirts) — historically high crime
Remote rural areas without local knowledge
natural_disaster_risks: Volcanic eruptions (Santa Ana, Izalco, San Miguel — all active)
Rip currents at surf beaches (major tourist hazard)
safety.political_stability_notes: President Nayib Bukele (reelected 2024) holds significant political power with high approval ratings domestically. State of exception suspending certain civil liberties remains in place. Democratic institutions under pressure. Political situation stable for tourists but monitor for changes.
common_scams: Taxi overcharging in San Salvador — use Uber or agree on price first
Inflated prices at tourist-facing restaurants in Zona Rosa
Unofficial 'tour guides' at Joya de Cerén and Tazumal ruins
Surf equipment rental overcharging in El Tunco
safety.police_reliability: Significantly improved under current administration. Police presence very high — visible and active nationwide. Tourist police in main tourist areas. Report incidents at nearest police station.

weather_and_seasonality.best_time_to_visit: November to April (dry season) — best weather, consistent surf, ideal for hiking and sightseeing
climate_zones: Pacific coastal lowlands — hot, humid, distinct wet and dry seasons
Central highlands (San Salvador area) — warm, pleasant year-round, distinct seasons
Western highlands (Santa Ana, Metapán) — cooler, more temperate
Eastern lowlands — hot and dry, harshest climate
weather_and_seasonality.hurricane_typhoon_season: June to November (El Salvador rarely takes direct hurricane hits but tropical systems bring heavy rain)
weather_and_seasonality.surf_season: April to October (largest Pacific swells); November to March (consistent smaller surf, offshore winds, cleaner conditions)

major_airports: Monseñor Óscar Arnulfo Romero International Airport (SAL) — San Salvador (only international airport, 44km from city)
airport_to_city_options: Uber ($20-30 to San Salvador center, ~45 min)
Shuttle services to surf towns ($15-25 direct)
transport.public_transit_quality: San Salvador has public buses but they are overcrowded and unsafe for tourists with luggage. Use Uber in the city. Pickups (flatbed trucks) serve as rural transport in coastal areas.
transport.intercity_bus_quality: First-class coaches (Tica Bus for international, King Quality) between major cities comfortable and safe. Local chicken buses cheap but crowded. Surf shuttle services between airport and coast towns are excellent and widely used.
transport.road_conditions: Pan-American Highway and main coastal road (Carretera Litoral) well-maintained. Secondary roads variable. El Salvador is small — driving the whole country in a day is possible. Roads significantly safer than pre-2022.
four_x_four_needed_regions: Ruta de las Flores mountain roads (rainy season)
transport.taxi_safety_notes: Uber widely available and recommended in San Salvador. If taking taxis, agree on fare before getting in — no meters. Airport taxis official and safe. Surf towns use mototaxis and pickup trucks for short hops.
transport.border_crossing_notes: Main crossings: El Amatillo (Honduras), Las Chinamas (Guatemala), San Cristóbal (Guatemala), La Hachadura (Guatemala). CA-4 agreement — 90-day stamp shared with Guatemala, Honduras, Nicaragua. All crossings open 24 hours but less safe at night.

budget.value_for_money: Excellent — El Salvador is one of the best value destinations in Central America. USD currency means predictable pricing. Pupusas are among the cheapest and most satisfying street foods in the region. Surf towns offer great value for the beach lifestyle.

connectivity.mobile_data_quality: Good 4G in San Salvador and main towns. Decent coverage on Pacific coast and main highways. Limited in remote western highlands and Morazán department.
connectivity.wifi_quality: Good in San Salvador hotels, cafes, and coworking spaces. Available in surf town hostels and restaurants. Variable speeds (5-50 Mbps). El Zonte has better connectivity than most surf towns due to Bitcoin Beach infrastructure.
best_remote_work_cities: El Zonte (Bitcoin Beach remote work community)
connectivity.power_reliability: Generally reliable in San Salvador. Occasional outages during heavy storms and in rural areas. Surf towns can have intermittent power — bring power bank.

best_areas_to_stay: Zona Rosa / Escalón / Santa Elena, San Salvador — safest and most upscale neighborhoods, good restaurants
El Tunco — most popular surf town, lively hostel and bar scene
El Zonte (Bitcoin Beach) — quieter, growing digital nomad and surf community
La Libertad — working fishing port, older surf scene
Suchitoto — colonial lakeside town, boutique hotels, cultural tourism
Santa Ana — western highlands base for volcano hiking
El Imposible / Apaneca — coffee highlands, eco-lodges
accommodation.peak_booking_season: November to April (dry season); Semana Santa (Easter) — extremely busy on coast
accommodation.accommodation_notes: El Tunco is the backpacker surf hub with best range of hostels and bars. El Zonte has attracted a digital nomad and Bitcoin community — more upscale and quieter. Suchitoto is the cultural gem — boutique colonial accommodation. Book Semana Santa accommodation months in advance.

local_customs: Salvadorans (Guanacos) are warm, proud, and resilient — the country's transformation is a source of immense national pride
Greet with a single kiss on the right cheek between women and mixed company; handshake between men
Family is everything — Sunday family gatherings are sacred
Strongly Catholic — religious holidays observed seriously
Bitcoin is a matter of national pride and curiosity — even skeptical locals are often interested in discussing it
culture.dress_expectations: Casual in surf towns and beach areas. Business casual for nicer San Salvador restaurants. Modest dress in churches and rural communities. Beachwear only at the beach.
culture.religious_sensitivity: Predominantly Catholic — Archbishop Óscar Romero (assassinated 1980, canonized 2018) is a national saint and deeply revered. His legacy is woven into the national identity. Semana Santa is the most important holiday.
social_norms: Salvadorans are direct and warm — conversation is easy
National pride is very high — the security transformation is a sensitive topic worth engaging positively
Pupusas are a point of cultural pride — always compliment them
Do not discuss gang issues casually — sensitive topic
culture.greeting_style: Warm — cheek kiss in social settings, handshake in business. 'Buenos días/tardes/noches' and '¿Cómo estás?' standard.
culture.public_behavior_notes: PDA acceptable in tourist areas and surf towns. Modest in rural communities. Keep a low profile with valuables.
culture.photography_etiquette: Generally relaxed. Ask permission before photographing people. Avoid photographing police in sensitive contexts. Ruins and natural sites freely photographable.
important_holidays: Semana Santa (Easter) — biggest holiday, coast towns pack out completely
El Salvador del Mundo Festival (August 1-6) — patron saint of the country, massive celebrations in San Salvador
legal_restrictions_tourists_often_miss: State of exception laws — police can detain without normal due process requirements. Carry passport at all times.
Photography of military installations and checkpoints not recommended
Drone permits required — DGAC authorization needed
Collecting marine life, shells, or archaeological artifacts prohibited
Bitcoin legal tender status — merchants technically required to accept it but enforcement is minimal

signature_dishes: Pupusas — thick handmade corn or rice flour tortillas stuffed with cheese (queso), beans (frijoles), chicharrón (pork), or loroco flower; THE national dish and one of the great street foods of the Americas
Pupusas revueltas — stuffed with combination of cheese, beans, and chicharrón
Curtido — lightly fermented cabbage slaw with carrots and oregano, always served alongside pupusas
Salsa roja — tomato and chile sauce, the other pupusa accompaniment
Yuca frita — fried cassava with chicharrón and curtido, popular street snack
Sopa de pata — cow's foot soup with vegetables and corn, traditional hangover remedy
Caldo de res — beef and vegetable soup, standard comida corriente
Tamales de elote — fresh corn tamales, sweeter than Mexican variety
Pan con pollo — chicken sandwich on crusty roll with vegetables and mustard
Riguas — fresh corn cakes cooked on a griddle, sweet and savory
food.street_food_safety: Excellent — pupuserías are the safest and most reliable street food in El Salvador. High turnover, fresh ingredients, hot off the griddle. Pupusas are a genuinely safe and exceptional street food choice nationwide.
food.vegetarian_friendliness: Good by Central American standards — cheese and bean (queso con frijoles) pupusas are outstanding vegetarian food. Many pupusa fillings are meat-free. Growing vegetarian options in surf towns and San Salvador.
food.vegan_friendliness: Moderate — bean pupusas without cheese possible. Growing options in El Zonte and El Tunco catering to international surf crowd. Challenging in rural areas.
must_try_drinks: Pilsener — the national beer, ubiquitous and refreshing
Kolashanpán — iconic Salvadoran soda, unique fruity/cola flavor; a national institution
Horchata — rice, cinnamon, and spice cold drink; Salvadoran version often includes morro seeds
Fresco de tamarindo — chilled tamarind drink
Atol de elote — warm sweet corn drink, comfort food
Café de El Salvador — excellent highland-grown coffee, particularly from Apaneca-Ilamatepec region
Chicha — fermented corn drink in rural areas
regional_specialties: Western highlands (Ruta de las Flores): coffee, mora (blackberry) preserves, locally made cheeses, artisan sweets
Pacific coast: fresh ceviche, mariscada (seafood stew), grilled fish
San Salvador: all national dishes plus growing international restaurant scene
Eastern El Salvador (Morazán): traditional indigenous-influenced dishes, corn-based foods
food.drinking_water_notes: Never drink tap water anywhere in El Salvador. Always bottled or purified water. Ice at established restaurants typically from purified water — ask in budget spots.
food.alcohol_rules: Legal drinking age 18. No strict public drinking laws in practice. Alcohol widely available except Good Friday.

top_national_parks: Santa Ana Volcano (Ilamatepec) — highest active volcano in El Salvador, stunning crater lake
Parque Nacional El Imposible — largest national park, cloud forest, excellent wildlife
Walter Thilo Deininger National Park — dry forest near coast, accessible from San Salvador
Cerro Verde National Park — dormant volcano with cloud forest, orchids, exceptional views
Montecristo Cloud Forest (Trifinio) — tri-country cloud forest at border with Honduras and Guatemala
Joya de Cerén Archaeological Site — UNESCO World Heritage 'Pompeii of the Americas'
Cinquera Forest Reserve — cloud forest, howler monkeys, civil war history

top_day_hikes.name: Santa Ana Volcano (Ilamatepec) Summit
top_day_hikes.description: Hike to the summit of El Salvador's highest active volcano (2,381m) and peer into a vivid turquoise-green sulfuric crater lake — one of the most dramatic volcanic crater views in Central America. On clear days, views of three other volcanoes and Guatemala.
top_day_hikes.notes: Access from Cerro Verde national park. Mandatory ranger escort — groups depart 11am daily when open. Check volcanic activity before going (periodically closes). $6 entry fee. Combine with Cerro Verde and Izalco views on the same trip.

top_day_hikes.name: Izalco Volcano Hike
top_day_hikes.description: Hike the stark, black cinder cone of Izalco — the 'Lighthouse of the Pacific' — one of the youngest and most active volcanoes in El Salvador. Challenging climb up loose volcanic scree with dramatic views. No vegetation on the upper slopes.
top_day_hikes.notes: Trail starts at Cerro Verde. Very loose black ash on upper section — trekking poles helpful. Check volcanic activity before visiting. Views of Santa Ana and Santa Ana lake from the top are outstanding. More strenuous than Santa Ana despite lower elevation.

top_day_hikes.name: El Imposible National Park Trails
top_day_hikes.description: Hike through El Salvador's largest national park — remnant cloud forest on the Pacific slope. Excellent birdwatching — black-handed spider monkey, agoutis, pumas, and over 300 bird species. Several trail options including summit of Cerro El León.
top_day_hikes.notes: Entry through SalvaNatura ($6). Guide recommended — trails not always well-marked. Only 4WD vehicles on the access road. Very remote feel for El Salvador. Name comes from the 'impossible' ravines that smugglers once tried to cross.

top_day_hikes.name: Ruta de las Flores Waterfall Walk
top_day_hikes.description: Walk between colorful highland coffee towns (Juayúa, Apaneca, Ataco) past waterfalls, coffee farms, and flower gardens. Juayúa's Cascadas de Juayúa are beautiful natural pools accessible on a short hike. Excellent food festival in Juayúa every weekend.
top_day_hikes.notes: Juayúa is the anchor town — weekend food festival (Feria Gastronómica) is excellent. Cascades are a 20-min walk from town. Combine with Ataco (art murals, handicrafts) and Apaneca for a full Ruta de las Flores day or overnight.

top_day_hikes.name: Montecristo Cloud Forest
top_day_hikes.region: Santa Ana department (tri-border with Honduras and Guatemala)
top_day_hikes.description: Hike through the most pristine cloud forest in El Salvador — the Trifinio Biosphere Reserve where El Salvador, Honduras, and Guatemala meet. Howler monkeys, quetzal habitat, bromeliads, and mossy ancient trees. Rarely visited, extraordinary atmosphere.
top_day_hikes.notes: Access from Metapán. Entry fee and guide required. Park partially closes during nesting season (May-October). Very remote and atmospheric. One of El Salvador's best-kept natural secrets.

top_day_hikes.name: Lago de Coatepeque Kayak Circuit
top_day_hikes.description: Kayak or take a boat on the stunning volcanic crater lake of Coatepeque — one of the most beautiful lakes in Central America. Clear blue-green water, surrounding volcanic ridges, weekend house culture. Swimming and lakeside restaurants.
top_day_hikes.notes: Kayak rentals from lakeside restaurants ($5-10/hr). Hotel Torremolinos has good access. Excellent combined with Santa Ana volcano and Cerro Verde in a western highland circuit. Crowded on weekends with Salvadoran families.

top_day_hikes.name: Tazumal and Joya de Cerén Archaeological Walks
top_day_hikes.description: Visit El Salvador's two most important pre-Columbian sites. Tazumal: impressive Mayan pyramid complex in Chalchuapa (Maya-Pipil). Joya de Cerén: UNESCO 'Pompeii of the Americas' — a Maya farming village perfectly preserved under volcanic ash from 590 AD.
top_day_hikes.notes: Joya de Cerén ($3) is one of the most fascinating archaeological sites in the Americas — you see actual preserved daily life, not just temples. Hire a guide at each site. Combine both in one day from San Salvador.

multi_day_treks.name: Ruta de las Flores Hiking Circuit
multi_day_treks.description: Walk or hike between the coffee highland towns of Nahuizalco, Salcoatitán, Juayúa, Apaneca, and Concepción de Ataco. Coffee farms, flowers, waterfalls, artisan markets, and excellent food. El Salvador's most scenic and pleasant multi-day cultural route.
multi_day_treks.notes: Can be done by bus and on foot between towns. Juayúa is best overnight base. Weekend food festivals in Juayúa add extra dimension. Roads between towns walkable. Excellent coffee everywhere.

multi_day_treks.name: Northern Mountains (Morazán) Circuit
multi_day_treks.description: Explore El Salvador's remote northern mountains and the civil war history of Morazán department. Perquín war museum, El Mozote massacre memorial, Río Sapo canyon hike, and the beautiful Cerro Pericón. Deeply moving and historically important.
multi_day_treks.notes: Perquín has a basic but excellent war museum. El Mozote massacre site is sobering and important. Río Sapo offers good hiking through river canyon. This is El Salvador's most overlooked region — authentic and rewarding.

volcanoes: Santa Ana / Ilamatepec (2,381m) — highest active volcano, spectacular turquoise crater lake
Izalco (1,950m) — 'Lighthouse of the Pacific', youngest and most active, stark black cinder cone
San Miguel / Chaparrastique (2,130m) — eastern El Salvador, active, last eruption 2013
Santa Ana range includes Cerro Verde (1,745m) — dormant, cloud forest, excellent views
Chinchontepec / San Vicente (2,182m) — twin-peaked dormant volcano
Conchagua (1,243m) — eastern El Salvador near Gulf of Fonseca
waterfalls: Cascadas de Juayúa — series of waterfalls and natural pools near Juayúa (Ruta de las Flores)
Salto El Malacate — impressive waterfall near Berlín
Los Chorros — natural spring pools and cascades near San Salvador (popular weekend spot)
Tamanique waterfalls — series of falls in coastal mountains near La Libertad
hot_springs: Aguas Termales de Ahuachapán — natural geothermal hot springs near Ahuachapán
Geothermal pools near Santa Ana — several small natural pools in volcanic area
lakes: Lago de Coatepeque — stunning volcanic crater lake, most beautiful in El Salvador
Lago de Ilopango — large volcanic crater lake east of San Salvador, site of catastrophic 536 AD eruption
Lago de Güija — lake on Guatemala border, archaeological petroglyphs
Laguna Olomega — eastern El Salvador, excellent birdwatching
rivers: Río Lempa — longest river in Central America running entirely within one country
Río Paz — western border with Guatemala
wildlife: Sea turtle — olive ridley and leatherback (Pacific coast nesting)
birdwatching_hotspots: El Imposible National Park — 300+ species, spider monkey forest
Montecristo Cloud Forest — quetzal, cloud forest endemics
Laguna El Jocotal (eastern El Salvador) — migratory waterbirds, jabiru stork
Cinquera Forest Reserve — howler monkeys, forest birds
stargazing_spots: Montecristo Cloud Forest (remote, no light pollution)
Ruta de las Flores highland towns (minimal light pollution)
scenic_drives: Ruta de las Flores (Sonsonate to Ahuachapán) — most scenic drive in El Salvador
Carretera del Litoral (coastal highway) — Pacific beaches and surf towns
San Salvador to Santa Ana via highlands

surf_spots.name: Punta Roca
surf_spots.description: El Salvador's most famous wave — a long, winding left-hander over a rocky reef at La Libertad point. One of the best left-hand point breaks in Central America. Consistent, powerful, and with proper swell can produce 200m+ rides. Has hosted world surf competitions.
surf_spots.notes: The crown jewel of Salvadoran surfing. Rocky entry and exit — booties helpful. La Libertad fishing port is the base. Stay in El Tunco (10 min) for better accommodation options. Best at mid to high tide.

surf_spots.name: El Tunco
surf_spots.description: El Salvador's most popular surf and backpacker town. Multiple breaks around a black sand volcanic beach including El Sunzal (consistent intermediate left) and El Tunco (beginner-friendly). Lively hostel, bar, and restaurant scene. Great for learning and improvers.
surf_spots.notes: El Sunzal break (5 min north) is cleaner and more consistent than El Tunco main beach. Surf boards and lessons widely available ($15-25/day rental, $30-50 lessons). Can get very crowded on weekends with San Salvador day-trippers.

surf_spots.name: El Zonte (Bitcoin Beach)
surf_spots.description: Quieter, more laid-back alternative to El Tunco with a consistent left-hander. Famous as the birthplace of Bitcoin Beach — the community Bitcoin circular economy experiment that influenced El Salvador's Bitcoin adoption. Growing digital nomad presence. Beautiful black sand beach in a cove.
surf_spots.notes: Better vibe and less crowded than El Tunco. Growing infrastructure with quality restaurants and accommodation. Bitcoin widely accepted in the village. Excellent base for longer stays.

surf_spots.name: Las Flores
surf_spots.description: El Salvador's best right-hand break — a long, peeling right-hander over a reef in eastern El Salvador. Far less crowded than the La Libertad area breaks. Classic surf camp destination with a remote, uncrowded feel. Good for intermediate surfers who want something different from El Tunco.
surf_spots.notes: 3-4 hour drive from San Salvador — remote location keeps it uncrowded. Several quality surf camps in the area. Combine with El Cuco and nearby eastern beaches. Black sand beach. Worth the drive for a right-hand break.

surf_spots.name: El Cuco
surf_spots.description: Remote eastern El Salvador beach town with consistent beach break. Long, wide black sand beach with fewer crowds than the western surf towns. Very local feel — few international tourists reach here. Good for exploring off the main tourist trail.
surf_spots.notes: 4+ hours from San Salvador. Very basic infrastructure — come self-sufficient. Eastern El Salvador is less developed but authentic. Combine with Las Flores nearby.

surf_spots.name: El Sunzal
surf_spots.description: Consistent intermediate left-hander between El Tunco and La Libertad — one of the most user-friendly quality waves in the country. Long rides, not as powerful as Punta Roca. Excellent for surfers progressing beyond beginner stage. Several surf schools base here.
surf_spots.notes: Stay at El Tunco (5 min away) for best accommodation access. Morning sessions best before crowds and onshore wind. Rocky reef — watch your fins.

wildlife_zones.name: El Imposible National Park
wildlife_zones.notes: El Salvador's most important protected area. Managed by SalvaNatura. Guide required. Only 4WD access. Remote and pristine — feels like a different country from the surf towns.

wildlife_zones.name: Montecristo Cloud Forest (Trifinio Reserve)
wildlife_zones.notes: Where El Salvador, Honduras, and Guatemala meet at 2,418m. Partially closed May-October for nesting season. Guide required. One of the most ethereal, ancient-feeling cloud forests in Central America.

wildlife_zones.name: Barra de Santiago Marine Reserve
wildlife_zones.notes: Large mangrove estuary and coastal reserve. Boat tours through mangrove channels. Sea turtle nesting July-October. Crocodiles visible from boats. One of El Salvador's largest mangrove systems.

wildlife_zones.name: Cinquera Forest Reserve
wildlife_zones.notes: Cloud forest reserve with civil war history — the forest grew back after being cleared during the conflict. Community-managed tourism. Howler monkeys audible from town. Combine with historical civil war narrative.

sports_and_activities.name: Surfing
sports_and_activities.notes: El Salvador is Central America's most surf-focused destination after Costa Rica. Consistent Pacific swells, warm water, and affordable costs make it a top surf trip. Punta Roca is world-class. El Tunco is the social and budget hub. Growing reputation globally.

sports_and_activities.name: Volcano Hiking
sports_and_activities.notes: Santa Ana crater lake is one of the most dramatic sights in Central America. Izalco's black cinder moonscape is extraordinary. Both accessible from Cerro Verde NP on the same day. Check volcanic activity — Santa Ana periodically closes.

sports_and_activities.name: Maya Archaeology
sports_and_activities.notes: Joya de Cerén is extraordinary — a Maya farming village flash-frozen in time by a 590 AD volcanic eruption. Actual preserved household objects, food stores, and gardens. UNESCO World Heritage. Much more intimate than Copán or Tikal.

sports_and_activities.name: Coffee Farm Tours
sports_and_activities.notes: El Salvador produces excellent shade-grown arabica coffee in the western highlands. Farm tours available from multiple operators in Apaneca and Juayúa. Buy direct from farms. Salvadoran coffee is seriously underrated on the world stage.

sports_and_activities.name: Bitcoin and Digital Economy Tourism
sports_and_activities.notes: El Zonte created the world's first Bitcoin circular economy before Bitcoin became legal tender in El Salvador. Walk around paying for meals, rentals, and services entirely in Bitcoin. Fascinating regardless of your views on cryptocurrency. The Chivo Wallet is the government Bitcoin app.

sports_and_activities.name: Colonial and Cultural City Tours
sports_and_activities.notes: Suchitoto is the most beautiful colonial town in El Salvador — cobblestone streets, artisan culture, Lago Suchitlán views, and excellent boutique hotels. Santa Ana has one of the finest neoclassical theater buildings in Central America. The national museum (MUNA) in San Salvador is excellent.

sports_and_activities.name: Sea Turtle Watching
sports_and_activities.notes: Olive ridley sea turtles nest on El Salvador's Pacific beaches July-October. Guided night tours with conservation groups available. Barra de Santiago is the best-organized ecotourism program.

sample_itineraries.one_week: San Salvador (1 day) → Santa Ana Volcano + Lago Coatepeque (1 day) → Ruta de las Flores (1 day) → El Tunco / El Zonte surf (4 days)
sample_itineraries.two_weeks: San Salvador (1 day) → Suchitoto (1 day) → Morazán / Perquín (2 days) → Santa Ana + volcanoes (2 days) → Ruta de las Flores (2 days) → Joya de Cerén + Tazumal (1 day) → El Tunco / El Zonte / Punta Roca (5 days)
sample_itineraries.classic_route: San Salvador → Santa Ana → Ruta de las Flores → El Tunco → El Zonte
sample_itineraries.offbeat_route: Morazán civil war circuit → Cinquera cloud forest → Suchitoto → Barra de Santiago mangroves → Las Flores eastern surf

practical_info.arrival_tips: SAL airport is 44km east of San Salvador — 45 min to an hour by car. Uber available from arrivals ($20-30 to Zona Rosa). Most surf tourists take direct airport shuttles to El Tunco or El Zonte ($15-20, pre-book). Many stay just a day or two in San Salvador before heading to coast.
practical_info.airport_scam_notes: Use Uber from the airport or pre-booked hotel shuttle. Official taxis from airport desk are safe but more expensive. Ignore informal taxi touts.
practical_info.local_sim_where_to_buy: Tigo and Claro SIMs at the airport and throughout San Salvador. Bring passport. $5-10 for a data plan. Claro has slightly better rural coverage.
practical_info.money_exchange_notes: El Salvador uses USD — no exchange needed for American travelers. ATMs at the airport and throughout San Salvador. No exchange needed.

laws.drug_laws_strictness: Very strict — possession of any amount can result in arrest. The Bukele government has zero tolerance approach. Do not carry or use drugs.
laws.alcohol_laws: Legal drinking age 18. No strict public drinking laws in practice. Alcohol widely available except Good Friday.
laws.drone_rules: Permits required from DGAC (Dirección General de Aeronáutica Civil). Prohibited over airports, military areas, and government buildings. Some restrictions near surf towns and beaches — check locally.
laws.wild_camping_rules: Generally permitted in remote areas with landowner permission. Not in protected reserves without authorization.
laws.protected_area_rules: Entry fees $3-6 at major parks. Guide required or recommended in El Imposible and Montecristo. No collecting plants, wildlife, or archaeological artifacts.
laws.lgbtq_legal_status: Same-sex relationships legal but no partnership recognition. No anti-discrimination protections. Social conservatism strong in most communities. Discretion recommended outside tourist areas.
laws.marijuana_legality: Illegal — strict enforcement under current administration. Zero tolerance.
laws.prescription_medication_rules: Bring prescriptions in original packaging with doctor's letter. Good pharmacy availability in San Salvador — many medications available OTC. Bring sufficient supply for time away from capital.
laws.bitcoin_legal_notes: Bitcoin is legal tender since September 2021. Merchants technically required to accept it but enforcement minimal. Most tourist businesses in El Zonte accept it. Chivo Wallet is the government app.

family_travel.car_seat_rules: Legally required but rarely enforced. Bring your own.
family_activities: Joya de Cerén — the real-life buried village is fascinating for curious kids
Ruta de las Flores — gentle highland scenery, food markets, flowers
Lago de Coatepeque — swimming and kayaking on a beautiful volcanic lake
El Zonte beach — calmer and safer beach environment than El Tunco
Macaw Mountain equivalent — Parque Zoológico Nacional in San Salvador
Los Cóbanos snorkeling — only rocky reef in El Salvador, accessible for families

accessibility.wheelchair_accessibility: Very limited — poor urban infrastructure, cobblestone streets in colonial towns, and unpaved access roads to most natural attractions make wheelchair travel extremely difficult.
accessibility.accessible_transport: Uber in San Salvador is the most accessible option. No wheelchair-accessible public transport.

gear.cold_weather_gear_notes: Light jacket and warm layer needed for Santa Ana Volcano summit (can be cold and windy at 2,381m). Montecristo cloud forest is cool and misty. Not needed at the coast.
outdoor_gear_shops: Surf equipment rental widely available in El Tunco, El Zonte, and La Libertad
Metrocentro Mall (San Salvador) has basic outdoor gear stores
Bring your own hiking boots — quality hiking gear shops scarce
